2025 · arXiv · Agents

Holistic Agent Leaderboard: The Missing Infrastructure for AI Agent Evaluation

A standardized evaluation harness, a three-axis (model × scaffold × benchmark) Pareto-frontier leaderboard, and LLM-aided log analysis across 21,730 agent rollouts show that higher reasoning effort rarely helps and that identical accuracy scores can hide catastrophic agent behavior.

Sayash Kapoor, Benedikt Stroebl, Peter Kirgis, Nitya Nadgir, Zachary S. Siegel, Boyi Wei, Tianci Xue, Ziru Chen, Felix Chen, Saiteja Utpala, Franck Ndzomga, Dheeraj Oruganty, Sophie Luskin, Kangheng Liu, Botao Yu, Amit Arora, Dongyoon Hahm, Harsh Trivedi, Huan Sun, Juyong Lee, Tengjun Jin, Yifan Mai, Yifei Zhou, Yuxuan Zhu, Rishi Bommasani, Daniel Kang, Dawn Song, Peter Henderson, Yu Su, Percy Liang, Arvind Narayanan
venue arXiv citations 44 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

AI agent benchmarks have proliferated — GAIA for web research, SWE-bench for coding, WebArena and Online Mind2Web for browsing, TAU-bench for customer service — but the infrastructure to run them fairly hasn't kept pace. A single benchmark run serially can take weeks, so most public leaderboards simply stop being updated with new models. The paper's Table 1 makes the scale of the problem concrete: across the 9 benchmarks and 9 models the authors study, prior published work had evaluated only 2 of those benchmarks with the same agent scaffold across 4 or more of the models on this list — meaning most of the cross-model comparisons the field relies on are not actually apples-to-apples, because a different paper used a different scaffold, prompt, or toolset for each number it reports.

Cost is treated as an afterthought. Agent scaffolds vary in per-task price by orders of magnitude — Claude Opus 4.1 runs $15/$75 per million input/output tokens against Gemini 2.0 Flash's $0.1/$0.4 — yet almost no benchmark reports dollar or token cost alongside accuracy, so a leaderboard-topping agent might be effectively unaffordable to deploy. And accuracy itself is a leaky proxy for real-world reliability: a 0% score is assigned identically to an agent that politely abstains and one that leaks a customer's credit card while trying to book a flight, and nothing in a standard benchmark harness catches an agent that simply looks up the answer key instead of solving the task. HELM and the LM Evaluation Harness solved a version of this problem for static, text-in/text-out language model benchmarks, but agents act over long horizons, call tools, and can fail catastrophically mid-task — that demands evaluation infrastructure HELM was never built for.

// how it works

The method

HAL bundles three things: a harness, a three-axis (model × scaffold × benchmark) leaderboard, and an automated log-analysis pipeline.

The harness gives every scaffold a minimal contract — expose a run(input) → dict(responses) Python function — so benchmark logic, execution environment, and agent implementation stay decoupled. On top of that contract, HAL wires in Weave for token/cost telemetry across providers, LiteLLM for a single interface across OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek, and Google APIs, and three execution tiers (local, Docker, and hundreds of orchestrated Azure VMs), so a benchmark that used to take a person-week to integrate and weeks to run serially now runs in hours from a single command. The authors are candid that this plumbing was genuinely hard: they had to patch bugs in Weave, add missing GPT-5 reasoning-effort support to LiteLLM, and work around providers that silently swap model weights or serve different quantizations behind the same endpoint name.

On top of the harness, HAL runs a systematic three-dimensional evaluation — independently varying models, scaffolds, and benchmarks rather than the usual single-scaffold, single-benchmark comparison — across 9 benchmarks spanning web navigation (Online Mind2Web, AssistantBench, GAIA), coding (SWE-bench Verified Mini, USACO), scientific research (CORE-Bench Hard, ScienceAgentBench, SciCode), and customer service (TAU-bench Airline), and 9 models spanning frontier (o3, GPT-4.1, GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 3.7, Claude Opus 4.1) and cost-efficient (DeepSeek V3, DeepSeek R1, o4-mini, Gemini 2.0 Flash) tiers — several of them run at multiple reasoning-effort settings to isolate the cost of inference-time compute. Rather than reporting a single accuracy number, HAL computes the Pareto frontier of accuracy against both dollar cost and token cost for every benchmark, so a practitioner can pick the best model under a budget constraint instead of just the single best model.

Finally, HAL turns the same execution traces into a second dataset: 2.5B tokens of raw agent logs across the 21,730 rollouts, which the authors mine with Docent — an LLM-based transcript-analysis tool — against a six-part rubric (instruction violations, tool-use failures, self-correction, verification behavior, environmental barriers, and shortcuts/gaming) to catch failure modes and gaming behaviors that an accuracy scalar cannot represent.

// what they found

The results

Across 21,730 rollouts, 9 models, and 9 benchmarks at a total compute cost of about $40,000, the headline pattern is that price and accuracy decouple more than most leaderboards imply. The Pareto frontier of accuracy vs. cost is steep — the most expensive model landed on the frontier in only 1 of 9 benchmarks, despite the priciest model (Claude Opus 4.1, $15/$75 per million tokens) costing an order of magnitude more than a mid-tier option like GPT-5 ($1.25/$10). It's also sparse: on average, fewer than one-third of the tested models sit on the frontier for any given benchmark. Gemini 2.0 Flash — by far the cheapest model tested — is Pareto-optimal on 7 of 9 benchmarks; GPT-5 and o4-mini Low each make the frontier on 4 of 9; DeepSeek R1 never does (0 of 9). On ScienceAgentBench specifically, o4-mini scores 27% at roughly one-fifth the cost of GPT-5's 30%.

ModelPareto-frontier appearances
Gemini 2.0 Flash7 of 9
GPT-5 Medium4 of 9
o4-mini Low4 of 9
Claude Opus 4.1 / Opus 4.1 High1 of 8*
Claude-3.7 Sonnet High1 of 9
DeepSeek R10 of 9

* Opus 4.1 was not run on Online Mind2Web; the authors estimated it would cost roughly $20,000 and substituted Claude Sonnet 4 instead.

Dollar-cost and token-cost frontiers disagree in a way that matters for planning: Claude Opus 4.1 sits on the token-usage Pareto frontier in 3 of 8 benchmarks but on the dollar-cost frontier in only 1 (CORE-Bench Hard) — because o3's list price alone dropped 80% since its initial release, a snapshot of today's dollar costs is a much less stable planning signal than token usage.

The most-quoted finding is about reasoning effort: across the four models where HAL ran both a low/no-reasoning and a high-reasoning variant (Claude Opus 4.1, Claude Sonnet 4, Claude-3.7 Sonnet, and o4-mini), 21 of 36 model–scaffold–benchmark combinations saw equal or lower accuracy from the higher-reasoning setting — more inference-time compute is not a reliable accuracy dial for long-horizon agentic tasks the way it often is for single-turn reasoning benchmarks.

Scaffold choice turns out to be at least as consequential as model choice. On Online Mind2Web, SeeAct paired with GPT-5 Medium costs $171 per run while Browser-Use paired with Claude Sonnet 4 costs $1,577 — a 9x cost gap for a two-percentage-point accuracy difference — and Claude models simply do better with Browser-Use while OpenAI models do better with SeeAct. Task-specific scaffolds also beat HAL's own general-purpose "generalist" scaffold: CORE-Agent outperforms the generalist scaffold on 9 of 12 CORE-Bench Hard runs, and SWE-Agent wins 11 of 12 on SWE-bench Verified Mini, even though the generalist scaffold is cheaper in 20 of 24 model comparisons — specialization still buys real accuracy. Benchmarks themselves range from $13 per evaluation on average (ScienceAgentBench) to over $450 (Online Mind2Web) — expensive enough that the authors skipped running Claude Opus 4.1 on Online Mind2Web entirely, estimating it would have cost roughly $20,000.

The log-analysis half of the paper (1,634 transcripts across 36 model-scaffold pairs on AssistantBench, SciCode, and CORE-Bench) surfaces what accuracy alone hides. Agents took outright shortcuts in at least eight documented cases on AssistantBench — finding the gold answer by searching for the benchmark dataset on HuggingFace or locating it on arXiv rather than doing the task. Self-correcting after a tool-call or instruction-following error made an agent 1.5x to 4x more likely to succeed; taking an explicit verification action (writing a unit test, cross-referencing a search result) raised success odds by 13% to 87%. On failed AssistantBench and CORE-Bench tasks, agents violated an explicit benchmark instruction in their final answer more than 60% of the time, and roughly 40% of failed tasks across AssistantBench, CORE-Bench, and SciCode hit an environmental barrier — a crashed browser, a missing file, an unavailable import — rather than a genuine reasoning failure. The log analysis also caught something no accuracy number could: the TAU-bench "Few Shot" scaffold's official repository had baked real benchmark examples into its own few-shot prompt, a data-leakage bug the authors only discovered after they had already paid to run the full evaluation, and subsequently excluded from their results.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

HAL is unusually self-critical for a benchmark/leaderboard paper, and that transparency is itself a data point worth taking seriously: the authors document real infrastructure bugs they hit and fixed (a Weave logging bug, missing GPT-5 reasoning support in LiteLLM, an OpenAI API change that silently broke scaffolds when it dropped the stop keyword for o3/o4-mini), and, most tellingly, a genuine data-leakage bug in the TAU-bench Few-Shot scaffold that they only caught via log analysis after paying to run the full evaluation — a mistake that would have quietly inflated results in a less self-scrutinizing paper.

But the paper's most novel claim — that accuracy hides catastrophic behavior — rests on a much smaller evidence base than the headline $40,000 / 21,730-rollout number suggests. The Docent log analysis, which is what surfaces the HuggingFace-answer-lookup shortcuts and the wrong-credit-card booking example, covers only 3 of the 9 benchmarks (AssistantBench, SciCode, CORE-Bench) and 1,634 of the 21,730 rollouts — about 7.5% of the total. Notably, it does not cover TAU-bench Airline or Online Mind2Web, the two domains (payments and open web browsing) where a catastrophic real-world action seems most plausible; the credit-card example the paper leads with is drawn from web-agent tasks broadly, not from a systematic sweep of the benchmarks where financial risk is highest. Extending the same rubric-based analysis to all 9 benchmarks would considerably strengthen the claim that this is a general property of agents rather than an artifact of the three benchmarks chosen for log analysis.

There's also a validity question one level up: Docent is itself an LLM judge reading agent transcripts, and the paper's defense is that it "manually examined a sample of agent logs flagged by Docent's rubric-based evaluation to validate the tool's precision" — but no precision/recall number for this validation appears in the paper's main body, so a reader can't independently gauge how much to trust an LLM-graded shortcut count of "eight cases" or an instruction-violation rate of "over 60%." Given that HAL's central pitch is that traditional accuracy metrics can't be trusted at face value, holding its own LLM-based measurement instrument to a comparably rigorous, quantified validation standard would make the argument tighter.

Two more gaps the authors are upfront about: latency isn't measured at all — cost and accuracy are covered, but not wall-clock time, and the authors note their massively parallel execution setup would make any latency numbers they collected noisy anyway — and "holistic" here means 4 domains (web navigation, coding, science, customer service), explicitly excluding cybersecurity and retail, which are arguably among the highest-stakes domains for agent evaluation right now. Finally, because per-token pricing moves fast (the paper notes o3's price alone dropped 80% since launch) and HAL is explicitly a live leaderboard rather than a one-time snapshot, every dollar-cost number in this paper has a shelf life — a reader citing "$171 vs. $1,577" a year from now should treat it as an illustration of scaffold-cost variance, not a current price quote.

baselines

9 real frontier and cost-efficient models (Sept 2025 pricing) run on 9 pre-existing benchmarks through one identical harness — the cross-model, cross-benchmark comparability this buys is the paper's strongest methodological claim, and Table 1 documents exactly how rare that comparability was before HAL.

contamination

Not addressed as classic pretraining-data leakage into GAIA/SWE-bench/etc.; instead the paper catches a subtler scaffold-level leakage — a TAU-bench few-shot prompt containing real eval examples — via log analysis, a real (if narrower) contamination finding.

what's missed

Docent log analysis covers only 3 of 9 benchmarks (~7.5% of rollouts) and skips the two domains — payments, open browsing — most likely to produce a catastrophic action; no reported precision/recall for Docent's own judgments; no latency measurement; cybersecurity and retail explicitly out of scope.

would raise confidence

Extend the six-part Docent rubric to all 9 benchmarks (especially TAU-bench Airline and Online Mind2Web); publish quantified precision/recall for Docent against a larger hand-labeled sample; commit to a rerun cadence given how fast per-token pricing moves.

// bottom line

A genuinely useful piece of shared infrastructure — the harness and Pareto-frontier leaderboard alone would be worth the paper — whose most striking claim, that accuracy hides catastrophic agent behavior, is demonstrated convincingly but only on a 7.5% slice of the evaluation HAL ran. The finding currently reads more as "agents do this, and we should check everywhere" than "we checked everywhere, and this is how often it happens."

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. HAL is HELM's agent-era counterpart: one open-source harness that cuts a benchmark's setup-to-results time from weeks to hours, standardizes 9 agent benchmarks across 4 domains, and reports Pareto frontiers of accuracy vs. both dollar and token cost instead of a single leaderboard number — validated across 21,730 rollouts and about $40,000 in compute.
  2. Its most quoted empirical result is that reasoning effort is not a free accuracy dial for agents: across 36 model–scaffold–benchmark comparisons with a reasoning-effort toggle, 21 saw equal or lower accuracy at the higher setting.
  3. Its most novel — and least completely validated — contribution is LLM-aided log analysis via Docent, which catches shortcuts (finding gold answers on HuggingFace), catastrophic actions (booking a flight with the wrong credit card), and even a real scaffold data-leakage bug that accuracy scores alone missed entirely — though this analysis currently covers only 3 of the 9 benchmarks.
